For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- World model: Green player at (42,12) in gray slot (38,8)-(43,13). Left panel has colored bars, checkerboard, and yellow square. Right panel has red/blue T-shapes. Clicking right targets toggles left objects horizontally. Clicking certain left objects triggers checkerboard swap. Arrow keys move green player within slot. Goal still unknown after 73 actions.
- Plan: Try moving green player outside the gray slot (down) to see if that's the goal.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 76 actions; a strong score needs about 59 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking on the red T-shape caused the checkerboard swap again. This is the same behavior as clicking on the blue T-shape.
I've used 76 actions and still haven't completed the level. I'm really stuck.
Let me look at the current state of the left panel. From the image, I can see:
- The red bar and dark red bar are now stacked vertically (not side by side)
- The blue block is still at the same position
- The checkerboard dots are at the same position
- The yellow square is at the same position
- The gray slot with green player is at the same position
Wait, looking at the image more carefully, the left panel now shows:
- Gray bar at top
- Red bar below it
- Dark red bar below that
- Blue block below that
- Gray bar with green player at bottom
This is different from before. The red bar and dark red bar are now stacked vertically, not side by side. This is a significant change.
Let me check the current segmentation to see what changed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state of left panel objects
for node in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if node['color'] in ['R', 'r', 'b', 'N', 'g', 'w', 'Y']:
        b = node['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in b)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in b)
        max_r = max(p[0] for p in b)
        max_c = max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"{node['color']}: bounds=({min_r},{min_c})-({max_r},{max_c}), px={node['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see something interesting. The red bar (R) is now at (24,18)-(29,21), which is above the dark red bar at (30,18)-(33,21). Before, the red bar was at (30,12)-(33,17) and the dark red bar was at (30,18)-(33,21), side by side.
So clicking on the red T-shape moved the red bar from (30,12)-(33,17) to (24,18)-(29,21). This is a significant change.
Let me think about what's happening:
- The red bar was at (30,12)-(33,17), which is 4x6 (rows 30-33, cols 12-17).
- Now the red bar is at (24,18)-(29,21), which is 6x4 (rows 24-29, cols 18-21).
So the red bar rotated from horizontal to vertical! And it moved to a different position.
This is a new mechanic I haven't seen before. Clicking on the red T-shape rotated the red bar from horizontal to vertical.
Now the left panel has:
- Red bar (vertical) at (24,18)-(29,21)
- Dark red bar at (30,18)-(33,21)
- Blue block at (34,8)-(37,11)
- Checkerboard dots at (20,18)-(23,21)
- Yellow square at (20,24)-(21,25)
- Gray slot at (38,8)-(43,13) with green player at (42,12)
Maybe the goal is to rotate the bars to match the T-shapes on the right panel. The red T-shape is an inverted T, and the blue T-shape is a regular T.
Wait, let me look at the right panel T-shapes more carefully. The red T-shape at (17,42)-(21,54) has 47 pixels, and the blue T-shape at (34,42)-(38,54) also has 47 pixels.
The red bar at (24,18)-(29,21) has 24 pixels (6x4), and the dark red bar at (30,18)-(33,21) has 16 pixels (4x4).
Hmm, the red bar is now vertical at (24,18)-(29,21), which is 6 rows x 4 cols. The dark red bar is at (30,18)-(33,21), which is 4 rows x 4 cols.
Together, the red bar and dark red bar form a vertical stack at cols 18-21, rows 24-33. That's 10 rows x 4 cols.
Maybe the goal is to arrange the left panel objects to form a specific shape. Let me think about what shape the right panel T-shapes form.
The red T-shape is an inverted T: a horizontal bar with a vertical stem pointing down. The blue T-shape is a regular T: a horizontal bar with a vertical stem pointing up.
Actually, I just realized something. The red bar is now vertical, and it's stacked above the dark red bar. Together they form a longer vertical bar. Maybe the goal is to form a T-shape on the left panel that matches the T-shapes on the right panel.
But the left panel objects are rectangles, not T-shapes. So they can't form T-shapes directly.
Unless... the goal is to have the left panel objects arranged in a way that "projects" to the T-shapes on the right panel.
Actually, let me try clicking on the red T-shape again to see if it rotates the red bar back to horizontal.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try clicking on the red T-shape again to see if it rotates the red bar back
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 19, 'col': 45}])
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
print(f"Board changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed', False)}")

# Check red bar position
for node in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'R' and node['pixels'] > 20:
        b = node['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in b)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in b)
        max_r = max(p[0] for p in b)
        max_c = max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"Red bar: bounds=({min_r},{min_c})-({max_r},{max_c})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Board changed: False
Red bar: bounds=(17,42)-(21,54)
